nodes.name: City Council
nodes.summary: The city council is the governing body of the city, responsible for making decisions and taking actions to address the lead levels in the water.

nodes.name: City
nodes.summary: The city is the municipality where the lead levels in the water have been found to exceed federal action thresholds.

nodes.name: Older Neighborhoods
nodes.summary: The older neighborhoods are the areas of the city where the lead levels in the water have been found to exceed federal action thresholds.

nodes.name: Resident
nodes.summary: The residents are the people living in the city, particularly those in low-income areas who are disproportionately affected by the lead levels in the water.

nodes.name: Emergency Infrastructure Replacement Program
nodes.summary: The emergency infrastructure replacement program is a $340 million program announced by the city council to replace the city's infrastructure over 5 years.

nodes.name: Community Groups
nodes.summary: The community groups are organizations that are taking legal action in response to the lead levels in the water.

nodes.name: Mayor
nodes.summary: The mayor is the head of the city government, who has acknowledged the failure of routine testing procedures and announced the resignation of the public works director.

nodes.name: Public Works Director
nodes.summary: The public works director is the official responsible for the city's public works, who has resigned due to the failure of routine testing procedures.

nodes.name: Older Housing Stock
nodes.summary: The older housing stock refers to the older homes in the city, which correlate with income levels and are more likely to have lead levels in the water exceeding federal action thresholds.

nodes.name: Infrastructure
nodes.summary: The infrastructure refers to the city's water pipes and other systems, which are being replaced as part of the emergency infrastructure replacement program.
